data.title: OptimizeRx signals $109M-$114M revenue target for 2026 as market shifts drive cautious outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: OptimizeRx Corporation (OPRX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Stephen Silvestro reported, "We delivered a strong fourth quarter, exceeding both consensus estimates and our internal expectations. Revenue for the fourth quarter was $32.2 million, and adjusted EBITDA was $12 million." He highlighted full-year 2025 revenue of $109.4 million and adjusted EBITDA of $24.3 million.
  • Silvestro explained, "Improvements in our product mix and channel partner strategy contributed to higher gross margins in 2025" and noted doubling of both adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow year-over-year.
  • Management is responding to softness in contracted revenue and a market shift away from managed services by updating 2026 guidance and focusing more on profitability. Silvestro announced, "For 2026, we expect revenue in the range of $109 million to $114 million and adjusted EBITDA between $21 million and $25 million."
  • The Board authorized a $10 million share repurchase program, to be financed through available cash and cash equivalents.
  • Silvestro addressed AI, stating, "Our business has experienced minimal disruption from AI, and we do not expect to be disrupted in the future...AI may serve as a tailwind."
  • Edward Stelmakh, Chief Financial & Strategic Officer, stated, "Gross margin increased from 68.1% in the quarter ended December 31, 2024, 74.8% in the quarter ended December 31, 2025. Year-over-year gross margin expansion is tied to a favorable solution and channel partner mix."
  • Stelmakh continued, "Our net income came in at $5 million or $0.26 on a fully diluted basis for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to a net loss of $0.1 million during the fourth quarter of 2024."

Outlook

  • Management projects 2026 revenue between $109 million and $114 million and adjusted EBITDA between $21 million and $25 million.
  • Stelmakh added, "Since we don't expect a similar revenue mix in 2026, our revenue phasing is likely to fall in line with historical 40% to 60% contribution between first and second half of the year."
  • Guidance language is more conservative, reflecting uncertainty around managed services and client spending patterns.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue was $32.2 million, with adjusted EBITDA of $12 million.
  • Full-year revenue reached $109.4 million, and adjusted EBITDA totaled $24.3 million.
  • Gross margin for Q4 2025 was 74.8%. Operating expenses for Q4 2025 decreased by $2.9 million year-over-year.
  • Net income for Q4 2025 was $5 million, or $0.26 per diluted share. Non-GAAP net income for Q4 2025 was $9.9 million, or $0.51 per diluted share.
  • Operating cash flow for 2025 was $18.7 million. Cash and short-term investments at year-end were $23.4 million, with a debt balance of $26.3 million.
  • Average revenue per top 20 pharmaceutical manufacturer was $2.8 million, slightly down from $3 million in 2024. Net revenue retention rate was 116%.

Q&A

  • Ryan Daniels, William Blair: Asked if conservatism in client spending is limited to companies in MFN negotiations or broader. Silvestro responded, "We're seeing a broader pause across all of the clients...contracting duration has started to shorten a little bit."
  • Daniels asked about D2C and HCP marketing pressure. Silvestro said, "It's about the same across the board."
  • Daniels followed up on AI's impact. Silvestro explained, "What it does is creates efficiency and speed within organizations."
  • Eric Martinuzzi, Lake Street: Inquired about percent of revenue under contract. Silvestro said, "We're running roughly 15% to 20% off of where we normally would be."
  • Martinuzzi asked about managed services. Stelmakh replied, "It includes very little. As we said last year, managed services is a very episodic solution for us."
  • Constantine Davides, Citizens: Asked about mid-tier manufacturer strategy. Silvestro described filling infrastructure gaps for these clients and noted growth "has exceeded our expectations."
  • Davides asked about capital deployment. Stelmakh said, "In most cases, I think you can expect us to spend that money on paying down the debt."
  • Jeffrey Garro, Stephens: Asked about first-half 2026 bookings and back-half dynamics. Silvestro stated, "We feel more confident in the back half."
  • Garro also asked about DAAP subscription mix. Andrew D'Silva, Chief Business Officer, indicated the company exited 2025 "pretty close to 10%" run rate for DAAP subscriptions.
  • David Grossman, Stifel: Asked about macro assumptions in revised 2026 guidance. Stelmakh noted, "Definitely a slower start to the year...as the year goes forward, these things will start to improve."
  • Grossman asked about net revenue retention changes. Stelmakh explained, "It's partially that...the conversion to a subscription model...smooth out the way revenues are recognized."
  • Davides asked about EHR and e-prescribe partnerships and exclusivity. Silvestro noted ongoing expansion and recent exclusivity wins with channel partners.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about client spending conservatism, revenue visibility, managed services, and macro uncertainties. The tone was probing and at times slightly negative, focusing on contract duration and client behavior shifts.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone, especially in prepared remarks, but was more cautious during Q&A. Phrases like "we think it's going to normalize" and "we're not panicking about that...it's going to adjust over time" signaled a watchful but optimistic stance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from highly confident and forward-looking to more conservative and focused on risk mitigation. Analyst sentiment became more pressing on near-term risks and revenue predictability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue and adjusted EBITDA improved sequentially, but guidance for 2026 was revised downward from the previous forecast of $118 million to $124 million to a new range of $109 million to $114 million.
  • Strategic focus shifted from expansion to risk management and preserving profitability, with less emphasis on new client wins and more on mid-tier clients and subscription revenue models.
  • Analysts shifted focus from long-term growth to near-term revenue softness, spending conservatism, and contract duration.
  • Management's confidence in the business model remains, but language is more guarded amid market headwinds and changing client behavior.
  • The share repurchase program and cost discipline were new developments not present in the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing softness in contracted revenue, driven by shifts away from managed services and conservative client spending amid MFN price negotiations.
  • Contract durations have shortened, and there is less revenue under contract compared to prior periods.
  • Gross margin expansion in Q4 is not expected to be sustainable throughout 2026 due to an atypically favorable mix.
  • Management's mitigation strategies include increased focus on mid-tier clients, DAAP subscriptions, cost optimization, and prudent capital allocation including debt reduction and share repurchases.

Final Takeaway

OptimizeRx delivered a strong Q4 finish and full-year 2025 performance, yet enters 2026 with a more conservative revenue outlook reflecting industry headwinds and evolving client behavior. Management emphasizes profitability, subscription-driven growth, and strategic capital allocation, while maintaining that the company is well positioned to capitalize on long-term digital pharma opportunities as market conditions normalize.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Owlet outlines 19%-23% revenue growth target for 2026 while expanding AI, telehealth, and international markets
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Owlet, Inc. (OWLT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jonathan Harris, CEO & President, highlighted, "2025 was truly a monumental and transformative year for Owlet, marked by significant growth and expanding our leadership and scale in pediatric health monitoring." He identified three pivotal achievements: the successful launch of the Owlet360 subscription service, surpassing 110,000 paying subscribers; the introduction of the Dream Sight camera with onboard AI; and record annual revenue, gross margin, and adjusted EBITDA despite tariff challenges. He announced the international launch of Owlet360, regulatory clearances for Dream Sock sales in India and Israel, and a focus on expanding subscription offerings and AI-driven features to increase customer lifetime value (LTV).
  • Harris stated, "Owlet expanded our share of total dollars spent on baby monitors to 41% in Q4 2025, up 24% versus Q4 2024 and another record high for market share since we started tracking Circana data."
  • Harris also outlined strategic priorities for 2026: driving global Dream Sock adoption, expanding the subscription platform, growing healthcare channels, and launching the Owlet OnCall Telehealth Platform. He noted, "We will launch the Owlet OnCall telehealth platform utilizing Dream Sock and Owlet360 to capitalize on this opportunity."
  • Amanda Crawford, CFO, said, "Q4 revenue was $26.6 million, up 29.6% year-over-year. Revenue strength was driven by broad-based growth across the Dream product suite and Owlet360 subscription."

Outlook

  • Crawford stated, "For the first quarter of 2026, we expect revenue in the range of $20 million to $21 million, gross margins of 50% to 52% and adjusted EBITDA of negative $2.5 million to negative $1.5 million."
  • For full year 2026, she provided, "We expect revenue in the range of $126 million to $130 million, representing growth of 19% to 23% over 2025."
  • Crawford added, "For full year 2026, we expect gross margins in the range of 49% to 52% and adjusted EBITDA in the range of $3 million to $5 million, representing growth of 50% to 150% over 2025."
  • She noted that guidance includes tariff cost impacts consistent with Q4 2025 and acknowledged uncertainties from tariffs and geopolitical events.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 revenue was reported as $26.6 million, a 29.6% year-over-year increase.
  • Full year 2025 revenue reached $105.7 million, up 35.4% from 2024.
  • Q4 gross margin stood at 47.6%, including a 510 basis point tariff impact. Full year 2025 gross margin was 50.6%.
  • Q4 adjusted EBITDA was $0.1 million, compared to $0.5 million in Q4 2024, with full year 2025 adjusted EBITDA at $2 million, improving $3.8 million over 2024.
  • Cash and cash equivalents were $35.5 million at year-end, with total liquidity of $45.5 million. The company paid down $12 million on its line of credit, reducing it to $7 million by quarter-end.
  • Total operating expenses in Q4 were $17.5 million, down from $18.4 million in 2024, while operating loss improved to $4.9 million from $7.4 million.

Q&A

  • Andrew Brackmann, William Blair, asked about Q1 guidance being below prior years and the ramp implied in the second half. Crawford responded, "We did observe some softness in consumer spending through the Q4 holiday period...retailers respond by tightening their weeks of supply, which is reflected in the Q1 guide."
  • Brackmann queried about AI insights and stickiness for Owlet360. Harris explained, "We're evolving from a simple hardware monitoring to real-time personalized AI sleep coaching...AI Sleep Insight will convert static data into actionable daily plans for parents."
  • Jungwon Kim, TD Cowen, sought details on international expansion and subscription learnings. Harris indicated, "We expect further international expansion revenue to begin the first half of this year...we are excited to drive that."
  • Kim also asked about guidance range expectations; Crawford clarified, "We have not baked in any like material contribution from our new countries or the telehealth opportunity. So we see that as upside in the guide."
  • Owen Rickert, Northland Capital Markets, asked about the OnCall Telehealth go-to-market strategy and its integration with subscriptions. Harris responded, "This will be an additional upsell, cross-sell, if you will, to Owlet360. It will be a separate platform."
  • Rickert inquired about subscription stickiness. Harris replied, "We've had 4 consecutive quarter of sequential growth across paying subscribers, MRR attach rate and retention rate."
  • Ian Arndt asked about long-term international revenue opportunity. Harris stated, "We have roughly 11% penetration in the U.S., meaning 11 out of 100 babies are actually wearing an Owlet sock...in Europe, we're closer to 3%."
  • Arndt inquired about the FDA safety communication. Harris said, "We have not heard anything further from that communication that went out in September of 2025."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts voiced cautious questions about guidance, international expansion, and new product stickiness, showing a slightly skeptical but constructive tone. Brackmann's inquiry about Q1 guidance and second-half ramp reflected concerns about seasonality and consumer spending. Kim and Rickert pressed on international growth and platform retention, indicating a desire for more granular data and confidence in growth levers.
  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking tone in prepared remarks and answers. Phrases such as "we are in a strong competitive position" (Crawford) and "we are excited to drive that" (Harris) illustrated high confidence. Responses were direct, with clear explanations for seasonality and macroeconomic effects. Discussion of AI innovation and subscription momentum was framed as a growth driver.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident but more defensive in Q&A, particularly regarding Q1 softness and macro uncertainties. Analysts were slightly more probing about guidance assumptions versus prior calls.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more specific, outlining revenue targets of $126 million to $130 million and adjusted EBITDA of $3 million to $5 million, whereas Q3 2025 did not provide full-year 2026 figures.
  • Strategic focus areas are consistent, but this quarter emphasized the upcoming launch of the OnCall Telehealth platform and expanding AI features, building on last quarter's early pilots and product launches.
  • International expansion progress moved from regulatory approvals and initial pilots in Q3 to clearances in India and Israel and the rollout of subscription offerings internationally.
  • Subscriber growth continues, with over 110,000 paying Owlet360 subscribers now compared to 85,000 in Q3.
  • Management tone shifted to acknowledge macro softness and consumer headwinds in Q4, with more discussion of tariff and geopolitical risks.
  • Analysts' focus evolved to deeper questions on revenue timing, margin impact, and the mechanics of new growth levers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited tariff volatility and the war in the Middle East as ongoing uncertainties impacting margin guidance.
  • Seasonality, particularly Q1 softness, was highlighted as a risk due to consumer spending patterns and retailer inventory management.
  • The FDA safety communication was mentioned, but Harris noted no material follow-up or impact to date.
  • Analyst concerns centered on the timing of international revenue recognition, the pace of subscription growth, and the sustainability of market share gains.
  • Management described ongoing efforts to mitigate risks by expanding regulatory clearances, investing in AI and telehealth, and maintaining disciplined cost controls.

Final Takeaway

Owlet delivered record performance in 2025 and is targeting 19% to 23% revenue growth in 2026, supported by momentum in its subscription platform, international market expansion, and the upcoming launch of the OnCall Telehealth platform. Management emphasized growing market share, operational efficiency, and leveraging AI to drive product innovation and customer retention, while remaining cautious on macroeconomic and tariff-related risks. The company is positioning itself as a comprehensive pediatric health data platform, aiming to capture long-term value across global markets and healthcare channels.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AudioEye outlines at least 30% adjusted EBITDA growth for 2026 with new AI-driven platform
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AudioEye (AEYE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Moradi highlighted "our 40th consecutive quarter of record revenue growth" and noted "we also demonstrated strong operating cash flow in recent years." He stated that adjusted EBITDA grew by approximately 35% to $9.1 million with a record margin of 22% for the year. Moradi announced that the integration of acquired customers is "now substantially complete, which should drive meaningful ARR acceleration in 2026." AudioEye released its next-generation platform, described as unifying "AI detection, expert audits and custom fixes in a single platform that delivers unmatched transparency, ease of use and 3 to 4 times of legal protection and other solutions." Moradi expects "adjusted EBITDA to grow by at least 30%, implying adjusted EBITDA of at least $11.8 million for the year." He also anticipates a "run rate adjusted EBITDA of $15 million by year-end, driven by AI efficiency across our products and operations."
  • CFO Kelly Georgevich reported, "Revenue again reached record levels with Q4 2025 revenue at $10.5 million, an 8% increase from Q4 2024 and a 10% annualized increase sequentially from Q3 2025." She further detailed that "on a full year basis, our revenue grew 15% to $40.3 million from $35.2 million in 2024."

Outlook

  • Management provided 2026 guidance: Q1 2026 revenue of between $10.5 million to $10.6 million, adjusted EBITDA of $2.2 million to $2.3 million, and adjusted EPS of $0.17 to $0.18. For the full year 2026, revenue is expected between $43 million and $44.5 million, with ARR growth expected to outpace revenue growth. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to reach at least $11.8 million, representing a 27% margin at the revenue midpoint. Management is "being pretty conservative," with expectations of "less nonrecurring revenue as we focus more on ARR."

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 revenue was $10.5 million, up 8% year-over-year and 10% sequentially. Full-year 2025 revenue totaled $40.3 million, a 15% increase from 2024. The partner and marketplace channel contributed $22.2 million for the year, with the enterprise channel reaching $18.1 million. ARR at the end of Q4 2025 was $40 million, a 9% increase from Q4 2024. Gross profit for Q4 was $8.3 million (79% of revenue), with full-year gross margin at 78%. Adjusted gross margin for Q4 was 85%. Net loss for Q4 2025 was $1.1 million or $0.08 per share, improving from a net loss of $1.5 million or $0.12 per share in Q4 2024. Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 2025 was $2.8 million or $0.22 per share, and full year adjusted EBITDA was $9.1 million or $0.72 per share. Free cash flow for Q4 was $2.3 million; for the year, $7.2 million. Share repurchases totaled $1 million for the quarter and $4.6 million for the year. Cash at year-end was $5.3 million, with $6.6 million in available debt facilities.

Q&A

  • Joshua Reilly, Needham & Company: Asked about human vs. AI involvement in custom fixes and platform differentiation. CEO Moradi responded, "The tools aren't really that good at accessible content because the internet wasn't coded with accessibility in mind ... So it's very far off to answer your question in my opinion." On differentiation: "No one has it right in the platform for the custom fixes. So that's the difference and we're using more and more agents with that as well to streamline it further."
  • Reilly followed up on revenue guidance puts and takes. Moradi explained, "We're being pretty conservative. The major factor is we expect less nonrecurring revenue as we focus more on ARR." Georgevich added, "If you look at the guidance for the year, it implies revenue growth of nearly 10%, and that's assuming lower nonrecurring revenue."
  • George Sutton, Craig-Hallum: Inquired about EAA investments and the EU opportunity. Moradi noted, "We are seeing pipeline building nicely, big deals in the pipeline, closed the big one in the fourth quarter and we expect to continue ramping up the EU as the year goes on."
  • Sutton also asked about AI and potential partnerships. Moradi stated, "We have very unique data. You can do a lot with that. I don't want to give away strategies on this call, but this data unlocks a lot of potential."
  • Zach Cummins, B. Riley: Sought updates on DOJ Title II enforcement. Moradi responded, "The DOJ's requirements are going to go into effect next month, as you said. We continue to see momentum on the reseller and even direct channels from states."
  • Cummins asked about gross margin expectations. Georgevich said, "Gross margin and adjusted gross margin, I think we expect to see relatively consistent to what we've seen ... with opportunities to see further growth in both of those different levers."
  • Richard Baldry, ROTH Capital Partners: Asked about customer adds and sustainability. Moradi cited, "That was a large reseller in the EU, the deal we signed in the fourth quarter that made up a lot of that."
  • Baldry also questioned sales and marketing investments. Georgevich replied, "We'll continue to invest in sales and marketing as long as we keep seeing that ROI, and we do expect to continue to invest in the EU as well."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were inquisitive about platform automation, revenue guidance, EU expansion, and AI differentiation, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone as they sought clarity on growth drivers and sustainability, without overt skepticism.
  • Management tone in prepared remarks was confident, using phrases like "we are providing the following guidance for 2026" and emphasizing record achievements. During Q&A, responses remained steady and informative, with Moradi reiterating a conservative outlook and Georgevich offering clarity on financial levers. There were no defensive or evasive responses noted.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment was consistent, with both management and analysts maintaining a focus on growth levers and execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize continued focus on ARR and reduction of nonrecurring revenue, with adjusted EBITDA growth of at least 30% for 2026, compared to an aspirational target of 30% to 40% annually previously.
  • Strategic focus advanced from platform migration to innovation in AI-driven accessibility solutions and completion of customer integration.
  • Analysts continued to focus on EU market momentum, AI differentiation, revenue visibility, and operational efficiency.
  • Key metrics such as revenue, ARR, gross margin, and adjusted EBITDA showed sequential and annual improvement.
  • Management confidence remained high, as seen in both prepared remarks and Q&A. Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, consistent with prior quarter.
  • Strategic priorities evolved with the launch of the next-gen platform and increased AI deployment.
  • Management’s confidence in leveraging proprietary data and AI models to drive future growth was more pronounced.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Moradi cited "increased rates of litigation utilizing AI to detect accessibility issues" and predicted "2026 will be the highest year of digital accessibility lawsuit on record."
  • Management acknowledged AI's dual impact, accelerating both business processes and accessibility failures.
  • The company is "being pretty conservative" in revenue guidance, factoring in lower nonrecurring revenue as a challenge.
  • The EU market, while promising, is described as "a bit bureaucratic" with potentially slower pipeline development.
  • Gross margin may face headwinds from "more AI compute," but management expects consistency with opportunities for improvement.

Final Takeaway

AudioEye management emphasized record financial performance, the successful rollout of its next-generation AI-powered platform, and a focus on driving ARR growth with conservative guidance for 2026. The company projects at least 30% adjusted EBITDA growth next year, underpinned by expanding U.S. and EU business, proprietary data advantages, and increased automation. Management remains confident about leveraging its differentiated platform and sees substantial opportunities ahead amid rising regulatory requirements and digital accessibility litigation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: TriSalus Life Sciences reaffirms $60M–$62M revenue guidance for 2026 while expanding PEDD portfolio
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TriSalus Life Sciences (TLSI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Mary Szela reported that "our results for both the fourth quarter and the full year were strong. Fourth quarter revenues were $13.2 million, and full year revenues were $45.2 million, representing a 60% and 53% increase, respectively, over the prior year periods." She emphasized the achievement of revenue growth guidance for 2025 and highlighted the commercial success driven by the expansion of the TriNav product suite and the PEDD platform into indications beyond the liver.
  • Szela announced the appointment of Michael Stansky to the Board of Directors, citing his experience in capital markets and governance as a strategic asset for future growth.
  • She detailed a public offering raising $46 million in gross proceeds, describing the financing as "more than 2x oversubscribed and... supported by experienced health care investors who share in our conviction in the long-term value of the PEDD platform."
  • TriSalus is prioritizing expansion of its sales and commercial infrastructure, aggressive investment in clinical studies to validate PEDD, and ongoing enhancement of PEDD technology across vascular anatomies. Szela stated, "we are reaffirming our revenue guidance of $60 million to $62 million" for 2026, with growth weighted to the second half.
  • The company has expanded its product portfolio from 2 core commercial products at the start of 2025 to 7 offerings entering 2026, aiming to drive more procedures per account and establish TriSalus as a single-source partner.
  • Launches included TriNav LV, TriGuide, TriNav FLX, and TriNav XP, with TriNav Advance anticipated in the first half of 2026 pending 510(k) clearance. The full portfolio targets a $480 million liver embolization market and collectively supports procedures in larger markets estimated at $2.3 billion.
  • CFO David Patience stated, "revenue was $13.2 million, representing a 60% year-over-year increase over the $8.3 million recorded in the prior period. Gross margin for the quarter was 87% and compared to 85% in the prior year period."
  • Patience highlighted a net operating loss of $3.3 million for the quarter, down from $7.6 million in the prior year, and an adjusted EBITDA loss of approximately $950,000, an improvement from $5.7 million in the prior year period.

Outlook

  • Revenue guidance for 2026 has been reaffirmed at $60 million to $62 million.
  • Management expects revenue cadence to be 40% in the first half and 60% in the second half, attributing the weighting to upfront commercial investments and timing of new product contributions.
  • Szela explained, “The revenue cadence will build meaningfully throughout the year as the realignment is completed. TriNav Advance has launched and the increasing productivity of the significantly expanded sales organization progress.”

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue was $13.2 million, a 60% year-over-year increase. Gross margin reached 87%, up from 85% in the prior year period.
  • Research and development expenses were $2.6 million, down from $3 million, primarily due to the completion of nelitolimod studies.
  • Sales and marketing expenses rose to approximately $8 million, while general and administrative expenses dropped to $4.2 million from $4.6 million.
  • Net operating loss for the quarter was $3.3 million, compared to $7.6 million in the prior year period. Adjusted EBITDA loss improved to approximately $950,000 from $5.7 million.
  • As of December 31, 2025, cash and cash equivalents totaled $20.4 million, with an additional $46 million raised in February via public offering.

Q&A

  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street Capital Markets: Asked about growth contributions from liver versus non-liver indications and clinical development plans. Szela responded the majority of 2026 revenue will still be liver-related, but expects "meaningful progress on the new applications" in the second half, tied to forthcoming data releases.
  • Takkinen: Queried about balancing growth cadence and EBITDA pathway. Patience said, "At this time, we're not providing specific timing or guidance and cash flow breakeven our adjusted EBITDA breakeven as we're just at the early stages of investing in our commercial expansion."
  • John Young, Canaccord: Sought detail on sales organization structure post-expansion. Szela declined to provide rep counts but confirmed the commercial organization is "meaningfully doubling" and has added a management layer for better coverage and targeted growth areas. Szela described a clinical specialist-rep pairing model for depth.
  • Young: Asked about mapping growth in 2026 guidance. Patience said XP and Advance products will have "a meaningful impact on our mapping" and expects growth supported by "studies that will be releasing concorded studies in the first part of the year."
  • Justin Walsh, JonesTrading: Asked about use patterns for TriNav portfolio. Marshak said some users request Flex "for every case," while others prefer a mix. On long-term liver vs. non-liver mix, Szela said liver penetration remains a major opportunity due to roughly "10% share" today, with foundational studies underway to prove PEDD technology’s superiority.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth drivers, path to profitability, organizational expansion, and product use patterns. The tone was neutral to slightly positive, with analysts probing for clarity on new application ramp and growth sustainability.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone in prepared remarks, repeatedly reaffirming guidance and the strategic rationale for investments. In Q&A, management was measured, providing as much detail as possible while withholding specifics on sales force size and EBITDA timing. Szela and Patience used language such as "we are reaffirming" and "we believe," signaling confidence but also some caution around timing and specifics.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management tone was more assertive on growth investments and commercialization, while analysts’ tone shifted to deeper inquiries on execution and new indication potential.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is now explicit at $60 million to $62 million, whereas the previous quarter only reaffirmed 50% growth for 2025 and deferred 2026 commentary.
  • Strategic focus has shifted to aggressive commercial expansion, portfolio broadening (from 2 to 7 products), and foundational studies for non-liver indications.
  • Analysts in the current quarter pressed for more clarity on non-liver growth, mapping, and organizational structure, while in the previous quarter, questions centered more on cadence, account penetration, and product evaluation.
  • Management's confidence is higher regarding readiness for a larger market and clinical validation, reflecting expanded resources and the recent capital raise.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the delay in releasing consolidated clinical data from the PERIO-03 study, noting that "this timing shift is not driven by safety concerns, efficacy signals or changes in our strategic priorities."
  • Upfront investments in sales infrastructure and commercial expansion may influence near-term revenue phasing.
  • There are continued challenges in timing of product launches and FDA clearances, as noted in the context of TriNav Advance.
  • Analyst concerns focused on execution of non-liver growth and the pace of scaling EBITDA.

Final Takeaway

TriSalus Life Sciences delivered strong quarterly and full-year results, driven by expanding its PEDD platform and commercial footprint. The company reaffirmed its 2026 revenue guidance of $60 million to $62 million and outlined a growth strategy centered on doubling its sales force, launching new products, and conducting foundational clinical studies. With a recently strengthened balance sheet and Board, TriSalus is positioned to capitalize on both liver and non-liver market opportunities, with key data releases and product launches expected to drive further adoption and growth in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Calidi Biotherapeutics plans public offering of stock, pre-funded warrants; shares down
data.description:
• Calidi Biotherapeutics (CLDI) announced plans to offer units consisting of common stock (or pre-funded warrants) and accompanying common warrants in an underwritten public offering.
  • Each unit will include either a share of common stock or a pre-funded warrant, along with a common warrant to purchase additional shares.
  • The securities in each unit will separate immediately after issuance.
  • The company plans to grant underwriters a 45-day option to purchase up to 15% additional shares and/or warrants.
  • Net proceeds will be used for working capital and general corporate purposes.
  • CLDI shares down 16% post-market.

data.title: Inuvo outlines shift to high-margin data products while targeting gradual platform recovery in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Inuvo, Inc. (INUV) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Rob Buchner emphasized a pivotal transition for Inuvo, highlighting the company's focus on leveraging its IntentKey technology amid industry-wide disruption and increasing regulatory scrutiny. Buchner stated, "I'm taking the helm as CEO at a decisive turning point for our company and for our industry at large," citing emerging agentic systems, increased privacy regulations, and the need for advanced quality controls as key drivers of change.
  • Buchner detailed four strategic pillars for 2026: a refined go-to-market strategy targeting CXOs for high-value deals, deepening integrations and partnerships to expand IntentKey adoption, raising the industry profile of IntentKey, and continuous product innovation to capture high-margin growth. He added, "IntentKey provides custom, adaptive audience models in real time, utilizing a large language model that doesn't depend on cookies or other legacy technologies... the real value proposition is in its speed."
  • The CEO reported early progress, noting, "we entered 2026 with the strongest sales pipeline for IntentKey we've had to date," and referenced the addition of 83 new clients in 2025.
  • CFO Wally Ruiz stated, "Fourth quarter 2025 revenue totaled $14.3 million, a decrease of $11.9 million or 46% compared to the fourth quarter of 2024." Ruiz attributed this to intentional moves in the Platform business, with cost-cutting helping to offset margin declines. He noted, "Operating expenses were $10.7 million, down more than 50% compared to the fourth quarter of 2024."

Outlook

  • Ruiz explained that Q1 Platform revenue is expected to "remain light with recovery coming gradually over the course of the year." He also forecasted "strong double-digit growth for each quarter of 2026" within agencies and brands, supported by a healthy sales pipeline.
  • Management expects continued investment in marketing for IntentKey and ongoing cost discipline, with Ruiz stating, "the SG&A, I think the G&A, the general and administrative will be -- will remain being flat to down in 2026. Compensation expense will be -- I suspect in our forecast is that it will be lower in 2026 than in 2025."

Financial Results

  • Ruiz reported, "Fourth quarter 2025 revenue totaled $14.3 million... gross profit of $9.5 million, a decrease of $12.3 million or 56% from the fourth quarter of 2024."
  • Operating loss for the quarter was $1.2 million compared to operating income of $220,000 in the same quarter last year. Net loss for the quarter was $594,000 or $0.04 per share.
  • For the full year 2025, revenue was $86.2 million, with a net loss of $5.1 million, an improvement from the prior year's net loss. Ruiz also highlighted liquidity events: "we ended the year with $2.8 million in cash and cash equivalents and $6.7 million availability under our borrowing facility... we received $6.2 million in connection with a class action settlement claim."

Q&A

  • Brian Kinstlinger, Alliance Global Partners: Asked about capital deployment between Platform and data businesses, and investments needed for new data opportunities. Buchner responded that marketing expenses are variable and tied to sales velocity, with capital for IntentKey focused on demand creation and supporting outbound campaigns.
  • Kinstlinger asked about raising IntentKey’s profile and expanding wallet share from 83 new customers. Buchner said, "having -- through thought leadership having more presence at industry conferences... and then it's also beginning to take our use cases to market, especially in some of these high-priority verticals."
  • Kinstlinger inquired about the expense outlook. Ruiz answered, "SG&A... will remain being flat to down in 2026. Compensation expense will be... lower in 2026 than in 2025."
  • Jack Vander Aarde, Maxim Group: Asked about the role of the IntentKey Self-Serve product in the growth roadmap. Buchner described it as a "longer-term ambition" with deals that are "almost pure margin."
  • Vander Aarde questioned breakeven targets. Ruiz responded, "We've always said that $25 million quarter we could be breakeven. And I think we're back to that again."
  • Vander Aarde also asked about Platform client relationships and contract extensions. Buchner stressed the importance of maintaining quality and compliance, referencing the launch of clicktransparency.org.
  • Bruce Hood, Excel Group: Inquired about a pending government contract. Buchner replied, "it's very much alive, and we're weeks away now... a multiyear, multimillion dollar engagement."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts voiced concerns about capital allocation, expense control, and the pace of Platform recovery, with a neutral to slightly negative tone reflecting cautious optimism regarding IntentKey’s potential.
  • Management maintained a confident, forward-looking tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly highlighting IntentKey's differentiation and the company’s readiness to capitalize on industry disruption. Buchner frequently referenced long-term ambitions and a "well positioned" outlook.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from defensive explanations for a compliance-driven slowdown to a more proactive stance on strategic transformation and technology-led growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from referencing a temporary slowdown due to compliance upgrades to emphasizing a gradual recovery in Platform revenue and accelerated growth in agencies and brands.
  • Strategic focus has broadened from stabilizing operations and completing technical upgrades to executing a multi-pillar plan centered on high-margin growth and IntentKey expansion.
  • Analysts in Q4 pressed for specifics on capital use, expense trends, and the timing of large contracts, while in Q3, questions centered more on product adoption and compliance risks.
  • Key metric changes include a sharp drop in Q4 revenue and gross profit, offset by improved cost controls and liquidity enhancements from a class action settlement.
  • Management’s confidence in IntentKey’s differentiation and pipeline has increased, with more clarity on strategic direction.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing challenges in the Platform business, noting, "the last couple of months has been challenging to the Platforms side of our business, even though IntentKey is showing robust signs of growth early in the new year."
  • The company is taking "prudent steps to contain costs where we can until the business returns to normative sales velocity."
  • Analysts raised concerns about the timing of government contract wins, the pace of Platform recovery, and the sustainability of recent cost reductions.

Final Takeaway

Management conveyed that Inuvo is executing a strategic pivot toward higher-margin data products and deeper integrations for IntentKey, expecting gradual Platform recovery and robust growth in agencies and brands for 2026. Liquidity has been strengthened through a class action settlement and new financing, supporting ongoing investment in innovation and marketing. The company remains focused on capital discipline, customer expansion, and leveraging proprietary AI technology to drive long-term resilience and shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BridgeBio Oncology Therapeutics, Inc. GAAP EPS of -$0.49
data.description:
• BridgeBio Oncology Therapeutics, Inc. press release (BBOT): GAAP EPS of -$0.49.
  • Cash Position: As of December 31, 2025, BBOT had cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities totaling $425.5 million, which is expected to provide cash runway into 2028.
  • Research and development (R&D) expenses: R&D expenses were $38.1 million for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $19.5 million for the fourth quarter of 2024. The increase in expenses was primarily due to increases in clinical trial expenses and manufacturing expenses for BBO-8520, BBO-11818, and BBO-10203.
  • General and administrative (G&A) expenses: G&A expenses were $5.3 million for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $2.3 million for the fourth quarter of 2024. Changes in G&A expenses reflect the initiation of BBOT's standalone operations and de-SPAC transaction.
  • Net Loss: Net loss was $38.8 million for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $19.7 million for the fourth quarter of 2024.
